elements.sent-2411-001: Categorical data composed of nominal valued attributes are ubiquitous in knowledge discovery and data mining tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Due to the lack of well-defined metric space, categorical data distributions are difficult to intuitively understand.
elements.sent-2411-003: Clustering is a popular technique suitable for data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, the success of clustering often relies on reasonable distance metrics, which happens to be what categorical data naturally lack.
elements.sent-2411-005: Therefore, the cluster analysis of categorical data is considered a critical but challenging problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper introduces the new finding that the order relation among attribute values is the decisive factor in clustering accuracy, and is also the key to understanding the categorical data clusters.
elements.sent-2411-007: To automatically obtain the orders, we propose a new learning paradigm that allows joint learning of clusters and the orders.
elements.sent-2411-008: It turns out that clustering with order learning achieves superior clustering accuracy, and the learned orders provide intuition for understanding the cluster distribution of categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments with statistical evidence and case studies have verified the effectiveness of the new ``order is all you need'' insight and the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-010: The source code of this submission is temporarily opened\footnote{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/OCL-demo .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Categorical attributes with qualitative values (e.g., $\{$lawyer, doctor, computer scientist$\}$ of attribute ``occupation'') are very common in clustering analysis on real datasets.
elements.sent-2411-012: As the categorical values are cognitive conceptual entities that cannot directly attend arithmetic operations~\cite{intro1 , most existing clustering techniques proposed for numerical data are not directly applicable to the qualitative-valued categorical data.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, the development of categorical data clustering techniques, including clustering algorithms, distance measures, and representation learning approaches, continues to attract attention in the data mining and machine learning fields~\cite{intro10 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the literature, there are two main types of clustering algorithms for categorical data: 1) $k$-modes and its subsequent variants~\cite{kmd}\cite{mwkm}\cite{woc that partition data samples into clusters based on certain distance measures, and 2) cluster quality assessment-based algorithms~\cite{sbcc}\cite{ecc that adjust the sample-cluster affiliations based on certain cluster quality measures.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Their performance is commonly dominated by the metric space represented by the adopted measures.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, the focus of subsequent research has shifted from designing clustering algorithms to proposing reasonable measures that suit the categorical data clustering~\cite{intro12 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=3.3in]{Figures/clustering_accuracy_distribution.pdf}} \caption{Clustering accuracy of $k$-modes~\cite{kmd} by treating 12 real benchmark datasets (detailed statistics are provided in Table~\ref{tb:statistics}) without orders (green bars), with randomly generated orders (blue regions), and with inherent semantic orders (brown bars). The two numbers in parentheses following dataset names report the number of attributes without and with inherent semantic orders, respectively. Original $k$-modes is directly applied to the former four datasets without orders. For a dataset composed of attributes with semantic orders, e.g., $\{$accept, neutral, reject$\}$, the Hamming distance adopted by $k$-modes is replaced by normalized order distance, e.g., $dist(\text{accept},\text{neutral})=0.5$, $dist(\text{neutral},\text{reject})=0.5$, and $dist(\text{accept},\text{reject})=1$. The random orders of attributes are generated 1000 times for each dataset to show the accuracy distribution by using the normalized order distance and $k$-modes. It can be seen that even random orders can easily achieve significant accuracy improvements.} \label{fig:pre_ex}
elements.sent-2411-017: The conventional Hamming distance measure and one-hot encoding strategy are very common in categorical data clustering, but they both simply represent the distance between two attribute values based on whether the two values are identical or not, which is far from informative.
elements.sent-2411-018: Later, on the one hand, more advanced distance measures have been proposed to discriminatively indicate the value-level differences by considering the intra-attribute probability of possible values~\cite{emd3 , inter-attribute relationship~\cite{adm}\cite{abdm}\cite{cbdm_journal , or both~\cite{jdm}\cite{udm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: On the other hand, advanced representation strategies that encode distances among data samples~\cite{sbc , and couplings in the attribute and value levels~\cite{cde}\cite{cde_conf , have also been proposed Although all the above advanced techniques have considerably improved the clustering performance, they are all task-independent and thus incompetent in adapting different clustering tasks on various categorical datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The learning-based approaches usually optimize the representation and clustering in a joint way to further achieve improvements in clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-021: Typical works in this stream include the distance learning-based methods~\cite{oc ~\cite{woc}\cite{ADC that learn the distance structures of the categorical values, and the representative learning-based methods~\cite{untie}\cite{mix2vec}\cite{het2hom that learn to encode the categorical data into numerical one.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the literature, a phenomenon that the possible values of a categorical attribute are with semantic order (e.g., attribute ``decision'' with values $\{$strong\_accept, accept, \dots, strong\_reject$\}$) has also been presented~\cite{intro2}\cite{intro3 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Such value order has been empirically proven to be crucial in obtaining appropriate distance structures and accurate clustering performance for categorical data~\cite{dlc}\cite{HDC .}
elements.sent-2411-024: It is noteworthy that order is the actual basis for clustering in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-025: For instance, when a $k$-means-type algorithm tries to partition a sample into one of the $k$ clusters, it actually obtains the overall order of the $k$ clusters in terms of admitting the sample.
elements.sent-2411-026: The overall order is formed based on the similarity orders of the clusters to the sample reflected by different attributes.
elements.sent-2411-027: Note that each numerical attribute corresponds to a one-dimensional Euclidean distance space with a clear order of values, which makes it easy to obtain the value order.
elements.sent-2411-028: According to the above analysis, we also note that although most existing categorical data clustering approaches do not specify the order of attribute values, their clustering process still passively forms the order through similarity measurement, regardless of whether there is an obvious semantic order of the attribute values.
elements.sent-2411-029: Therefore, knowing the correct order of attribute values may be beneficial to categorical data clustering, and the experimental results in Figure~\ref{fig:pre_ex confirmed this.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It can be seen that even randomly generated orders can easily achieve significant accuracy improvements.
elements.sent-2411-031: Another interesting observation is that, although attribute values of some datasets (i.e., the right eight datasets) are with obvious semantic orders, they are rarely optimal for clustering.
elements.sent-2411-032: This is because the semantic orders of attributes are relatively independent of specific clustering tasks, and this information gap can prevent some semantic orders from well-serving clustering.
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, obtaining the optimal orders w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-034: This paper, therefore, proposes a new clustering paradigm that dynamically learns orders based on the current data partition during clustering.
elements.sent-2411-035: Since the order learning is connected to the clustering task, the optimal order for clustering can thus be automatically obtained.
elements.sent-2411-036: To make full use of the learned orders, a distance learning mechanism is also introduced to dynamically measure the sample-cluster affiliation under the guidance of the orders.
elements.sent-2411-037: It turns out that the learned orders can significantly improve clustering accuracy and can also intuitively help understand the distribution and clustering effect of categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-038: Extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-039: The main contributions can be summarized into three points:.
elements.sent-2411-040: A new insight that ``obtaining the optimal orders is the decisive factor for accurately clustering categorical data'' is proposed for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-041: Accordingly, a new clustering paradigm with order distance learning is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose a distance structure learning mechanism to automatically obtain the order and distance.
elements.sent-2411-043: This learning process is jointly performed with clustering, making the clustering performance more robust to various datasets.
elements.sent-2411-044: Both the learning process and the learned order are highly interpretable, which is helpful for understanding complex categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, the algorithm is efficient, strictly convergent, and can be easily extended to mixed data\footnote{Dataset composed of a mixture of categorical and numerical attributes.. .}
elements.sent-2411-046: This section overviews the related works in data encoding, representation learning, and consideration of value order, for categorical data clustering.
elements.sent-2411-047: The data encoding techniques convert the categorical data into numerical values based on certain strategies, and then perform clustering on the numerical encoding.
elements.sent-2411-048: The conventional one-hot encoding and Hamming distance construct similar distance structures for categorical data by assigning distances ``1'' and ``0'' to any pair of different and identical values, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-049: As they cannot distinguish different dissimilarity degrees~\cite{intro10}\cite{intro6 of data samples, information loss is usually unavoidable during clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Some statistic-based distance measures~\cite{lsm}\cite{oc}\cite{intro10 consider the intra-attribute occurrence frequency of values to better distinguish the data samples.}
elements.sent-2411-051: They take a common principle that two similar values may have more consistent statistics within the corresponding attribute or cluster.
elements.sent-2411-052: Some metrics~\cite{adm}\cite{abdm further define distance considering the relevance between attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The representative measure proposed in~\cite{cbdm_journal selects more relevant attributes for the distance measurement.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Later, the measures~\cite{jdm}\cite{cms that more comprehensively consider both the intra- and inter-attribute couplings reflected by the data statistics have been presented.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Coupling-based encoding strategies~\cite{cde}\cite{cde_conf have also been designed accordingly to encode the couplings.}
elements.sent-2411-056: All the above techniques perform value-level encoding to reflect sample-level distances, while the work~\cite{sbc directly encodes a data sample by concatenating the distances from it to all the other samples.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Recently, more advanced representation learning methods have been proposed to learn the categorical data encoding.
elements.sent-2411-058: The work proposed in~\cite{untie projects dataset through different kernels for more informative coupling representation, and then learns the kernel parameters to obtain task-oriented representation.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Some approaches~\cite{mai}\cite{mix2vec have also been designed to simultaneously encode the numerical and categorical attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, the involved hyper-parameters have a great impact on their performance, and the appropriate parameter selection will be laborious in different real applications.
elements.sent-2411-061: Although a parameter-free learning approach~\cite{woc has been proposed to learn sample-cluster distance and number of clusters simultaneously, the distance is updated dependent on cluster statistics and thus cannot reveal the natural distance structure of categorical data.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Some new advances proposed with the consideration of value order further distinguish categorical attributes into nominal and ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-063: The distance metrics~\cite{ebdmjournal}\cite{udm define the distance between attribute values based on the data statistical information with considering the semantic order.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Later, a representation learning approach was introduced by~\cite{dlc , which treats the possible values of an ordinal attribute as linearly arranged concepts, and learns the distances between adjacent concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Later, the works~\cite{HDC}\cite{ADC further unify the distances of nominal and ordinal attributes, and make them learnable with clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The most recent work~\cite{het2hom attempts to convert the nominal attributes into ordinal ones through geometric projection, and then learn the latent distance spaces during clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The above recent advances achieve considerable clustering performance improvements and empirically prove the importance of value order in categorical data clustering.
elements.sent-2411-068: However, they all assume the availability of semantic order of attribute values.
elements.sent-2411-069: Moreover, they directly adopt this order for clustering, and overlook that the semantic order may not be suitable under different clustering scenarios as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:pre_ex .}
elements.sent-2411-070: This section first formulates the problem, then proposes the order distance definition and order learning mechanism, and finally introduces the joint learning of the orders and clusters.
elements.sent-2411-071: Analyses of time complexity and convergence are also provided.
elements.sent-2411-072: The problem of categorical data clustering with order learning is formulated as follows.

elements.sent-2411-091: For a crisp partitional clustering task to partition $X$ into $k$ non-overlapping subsets $C=\{C_1, C_2,..., C_k\}$, the objective can be formalized as the problem of minimizing: \begin{equation}\label{eq:obj_pre} L(\mathbf{Q},O)=\sum_{m=1}^k\sum_{i=1}^nq_{i,m}\cdot\Theta(\mathbf{x}_i,C_m;O)\end{equation} where $\mathbf{Q}$ is an $n\times k$ matrix with its $(i,m)$-th entry $q_{i,m}$ indicating the affiliation between sample $\mathbf{x _i$ and cluster $C_m$.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{OCL: Order and Cluster Learning Algorithm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \label{alg:OCL} \REQUIRE Dataset $X$, number of sought clusters $k$ \ENSURE Partition $\mathbf{Q}$, order $O$ \STATE Initialization: Set outer loop counter and convergence mark by $E\leftarrow 0$ and $Conv\_O\leftarrow False$, respectively; Randomly partition $X$ into $k$ clusters $C$, obtain $\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$ and $\mathbf{P}^{\{I\}}$ accordingly; Randomly initialize $O^{\{E\}}$, update $\mathbf{D}^{\{E\}}$ accordingly; \WHILE{$Conv\_O=False$} \STATE $E\leftarrow E+1$; Compute $O^{\{E\}}$ by Eqs.~(\ref{eq:relax_order}) - (\ref{eq:weight_mean}); Update $\mathbf{D}^{\{E\}}$ accordingly; Set inner loop counter and convergence mark by $I\leftarrow 0$ and $Conv\_I\leftarrow False$, respectively; \WHILE{$Conv\_I=False$} \STATE $I\leftarrow I+1$; Compute $\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$ by Eq.~(\ref{eq:qim}); Update $\mathbf{P}^{\{I\}}$ accordingly; Obtain $L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}},O^{\{E\}})$; \IF {$L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}},O^{\{E\}})\geq L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I-1\}},O^{\{E\}})$} \STATE $Conv\_I\leftarrow True$; $\mathbf{Q}^{\{E\}}=\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$; \ENDIF \ENDWHILE \IF {$L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{E\}},O^{\{E\}})\geq L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{E-1\}},O^{\{E-1\}})$} \STATE $Conv\_O\leftarrow True$; \ENDIF \ENDWHILE \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-196: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the core components of OCL and to verify our hypothesis on the orders of categorical data attributes, we design several variants of OCL for ablation studies from both algorithm and data perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-197: From the algorithm aspect, to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Order Distance (OD), we compare OCL with its variant OCL$^I$, which uses normalized equidistant order distance without considering the probability distribution of possible values.
elements.sent-2411-198: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Order Learning (OL) mechanism, we further remove the process of order learning from OCL$^I$ and only update the order once in the first iteration, which forms another variant OCL$^{II}$.
elements.sent-2411-199: To completely discard Order Information (OI), we further make OCL$^{II}$ use only the traditional Hamming distance, thus forming the third variant OCL$^{III}$.
elements.sent-2411-200: Table \ref{tb:ab_ca compares the clustering performance of OCL and the three variants.}
elements.sent-2411-201: It can be observed that the performance of OCL is superior to all variants.
elements.sent-2411-202: More specifically, OCL outperforms OCL$^I$ significantly on 11 out of all 12 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-203: This indicates that OD can effectively utilize the distribution information of the attribute values within different clusters for reasonable order distance measurement.
elements.sent-2411-204: Furthermore, the performance of OCL$^I$ is also significantly better than OCL$^{II}$ on all 12 datasets, which verifies the effectiveness of the OL mechanism in searching optimal orders during clustering.
elements.sent-2411-205: In addition, compared to the OCL$^{III}$ variant that completely disregards OI, OCL$^{II}$ has better performance on 11 out of all 12 datasets, which proves that even a roughly estimated initial order can still significantly and extensively improve clustering performance.

elements.sent-2411-229: This paper introduces a new finding that knowing the correct order relation of attribute values is critical to cluster analysis of categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-230: Accordingly, we propose the OCL paradigm that allows joint learning of data partitions and order relation of attribute values for accurate clustering.
elements.sent-2411-231: The proposed learning mechanism can infer a more appropriate order among categorical attribute values based on the current clusters, thus yielding more compact clusters based on the updated order.
elements.sent-2411-232: The data partition and order learning are iteratively executed to approach the clustering objective, i.e., maximizing the overall similarity of intra-cluster samples.
elements.sent-2411-233: As a result, more accurate clustering results and insightful orders can be obtained simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-234: OCL converges quickly with guarantee, and extensive experiments conducted on 12 real benchmark datasets fully demonstrate its superiority in comparison with state-of-the-art clustering methods.
elements.sent-2411-235: A case study has also been provided to show the rationality of the learned order in understanding unlabeled categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-236: The proposed OCL method is also not exempt from limitations.
elements.sent-2411-237: Although it demonstrates the potential to enhance the performance of mixed data clustering, the deep connection between the treatment of categorical and numerical attributes remains to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-238: In addition, it would also be promising to extend OCL to more complex datasets with non-static distribution, an unknown number of clusters, and extremely imbalanced clusters.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Categorical data composed of qualitative valued attributes are ubiquitous in machine learning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-240: Due to the lack of well-defined metric space, categorical data distributions are difficult to be intuitively understood.
elements.sent-2411-241: Clustering is a popular data analysis technique suitable for data distribution understanding.
elements.sent-2411-242: This paper therefore introduces a new finding that the order relation among attribute values is the decisive factor in clustering accuracy, and is also the key to understanding categorical data clusters, because the essence of clustering is to order the clusters in terms of their admission to samples.
elements.sent-2411-243: To obtain the orders, we propose a new learning paradigm that allows joint learning of clusters and the orders.
elements.sent-2411-244: It alternatively partitions the data into clusters based on the distance metric built upon the orders and estimates the most likely orders according to the clusters.
elements.sent-2411-245: The algorithm achieves superior clustering accuracy with a convergence guarantee, and the learned orders facilitate the understanding of the non-intuitive cluster distribution of categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-246: Extensive experiments with ablation studies, statistical evidence, and case studies have validated the new insight into the importance of value order and the method proposition.

elements.sent-2411-262: The main contributions of this work are summarized into three points:.
elements.sent-2411-263: A new insight that ``obtaining the optimal orders is the decisive factor for accurately clustering categorical data'' is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-264: Accordingly, a new clustering paradigm with order distance learning is presented.
elements.sent-2411-265: We propose an iterative learning mechanism to realize the optimization of the order and distance.
elements.sent-2411-266: This learning process is jointly performed with clustering, making the clustering performance more adaptive to various clustering scenarios $T(k,X)$.
elements.sent-2411-267: Both the learning process and learned orders are highly interpretable, which helps understand complex categorical data.

elements.sent-2411-300: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the core components of OCL and to verify our hypothesis on the orders of categorical data attributes, several ablated variants of OCL are compared from both algorithm and data perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-301: From the algorithm aspect, to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Order Distance (OD), we compare OCL with its variant OCL-I, which uses normalized equidistant order distance without considering the probability distribution of possible values.
elements.sent-2411-302: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Order Learning (OL) mechanism, we further remove the process of order learning from OCL-I and only update the order once in the first iteration, which forms OCL-II.
elements.sent-2411-303: To completely discard Order Information (OI), we further make OCL-II use only the traditional Hamming distance, thus forming OCL-III.
elements.sent-2411-304: Table \ref{tb:ab_ca compares the three OCL variants.}
elements.sent-2411-305: More specifically, OCL outperforms OCL-I significantly on 11 out of all 12 datasets, indicating that OD can effectively utilize the distribution information of the attribute values within different clusters for reasonable order distance measurement.
elements.sent-2411-306: Furthermore, the performance of OCL-I is also significantly better than OCL-II on all 12 datasets, which verifies the effectiveness of the OL mechanism in searching optimal orders during clustering.
elements.sent-2411-307: In addition, compared to OCL-III that completely disregards OI, OCL-II has better performance on 11 out of all 12 datasets, which proves that even a roughly estimated initial order can still effectively improve clustering performance.

elements.sent-2411-319: This paper introduces and validates a new finding that knowing the correct order relation of attribute values is critical to cluster analysis of categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-320: Accordingly, the OCL paradigm is proposed allowing joint learning of data partitions and order relation-guided distances.
elements.sent-2411-321: Its learning mechanism can infer a more appropriate order among attribute values based on the current clusters, thus yielding more compact clusters based on the updated order.
elements.sent-2411-322: The data partition and order learning are iteratively executed to approach the optimal clustering solution.
elements.sent-2411-323: As a result, more accurate clustering results and insightful orders can be obtained.
elements.sent-2411-324: OCL converges quickly with guarantee, and extensive experiments on 12 real benchmark datasets fully demonstrate its superiority in comparison with state-of-the-art clustering methods.
elements.sent-2411-325: A case study has also been provided to show the rationality of the learned order in understanding categorical data clusters.
elements.sent-2411-326: The proposed OCL is also not exempt from limitations.
elements.sent-2411-327: For instance, the natural connection between the treatment of categorical and numerical attributes remains to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-328: It would also be promising to extend OCL to more complex scenarios, e.g., non-stationary mixed dataset with an unknown number of imbalanced clusters.

elements.subsection-2411-017: Ablation Study Evaluated by ARI and NMI
elements.sent-2411-361: The ablation study compares different ablated variants of OCL from the algorithm and data perspectives using the ARI and NMI indices, and the results are demonstrated in Table~\ref{tb:ab_ARI - Table~\ref{tb:ab_nmi_d .}}
elements.sent-2411-363: From the algorithm aspect, it can be observed that the performance of OCL is superior to all variants on both ARI and NMI indices.
elements.sent-2411-364: More specifically, OCL outperforms OCL-I significantly on 10 out of 12 datasets both on ARI and NMI indices, and its performance is similar to OCL-I on the other 2 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-365: This indicates that using the probability-based order distance structure can effectively utilize the intra-cluster distribution information for more reasonable clustering-oriented distance measurement.
elements.sent-2411-366: Furthermore, the performance of OCL-I is also significantly better than OCL-II on 10 out of 12 datasets, which verifies the effectiveness of the order learning mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-367: In addition, compared to the OCL-III variant that completely disregards order information, OCL-II has better performance on all 12 datasets, which proves the effectiveness of considering orders for clustering.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Efficiency Evaluation on Nursery School (NS) Dataset
elements.sent-2411-373: To make a more comprehensive evaluation of the execution efficiency of OCL, we also sample the real benchmark NS dataset with sampling rates from 50\% to 500\% with a step size of 50\%, to simultaneously investigate the execution time in terms of $n$ and the clustering accuracy of OCL.
elements.sent-2411-374: Please note that the labels of samples are also sampled, and are only used to evaluate clustering accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-375: Since sampling attributes are meaningless for evaluating clustering accuracy, only the results w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-376: The results are reported in Figure~\ref{fig:NS_time .}
elements.sent-2411-377: Overall, the execution time of OCL is better than UDMC and AMPHM on different-sized NS datasets.
elements.sent-2411-378: It can also be observed that the clustering accuracy of the proposed OCL method is stable under different sample scale, and is always higher than the compared methods.
elements.sent-2411-379: Additionally, as the number of samples increases, the execution time of OCL increases linearly, which is consistent with our time complexity analysis in Section~\ref{sct:theoretical_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-380: In summary, OCL is effective and efficient compared to the state-of-the-art methods and does not incur too much additional computational cost compared to the simplest methods.

elements.sent-2411-381: It is worth noting that the proposed order learning method not only improves the clustering performance but also mines the hidden order information when facing datasets with unclear semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-382: This case study investigates whether the value order learned by the proposed method is interpretable and consistent with human subjective understanding.
elements.sent-2411-383: The Hayes-Roth dataset with one nominal and three ordinal attributes records personal information, such as the nominal ``Hobby'', ordinal ``Education level'', ``Age'', and ``Marital status''.
elements.sent-2411-384: The true labels of this dataset divide the samples into three groups of club members for psychological evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-385: Original dataset information is visualized in Figures~\ref{fig:case_study (a).}
elements.sent-2411-386: It can be seen from Figure~\ref{fig:case_study (b) that our method can correctly learn the semantic order of the values from attributes ``Education level'' and ``Age'', as the learned orders are consistent with the original semantic orders.}
elements.sent-2411-387: Moreover, for the nominal attribute ``Hobby'', although there is no original obvious semantic order, we show below that the learned order is very meaningful in data analysis and knowledge acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-388: First, we can find that the learned order distance between ``chess'' and ``stamp collection'' hobbies is closer, while the distance between ``stamp collection'' and ``sport'' is larger.
elements.sent-2411-389: This is consistent with a cognitive intuition that ``chess'' is a quieter sport, so it ranks between the quieter hobby ``stamp collection'' and its sibling ``sport''.
elements.sent-2411-390: This indicates that OCL can learn latent semantic orders for nominal attributes, which is an important characteristic for categorical data understanding.
elements.sent-2411-391: As shown in the clustering results obtained by OCL in Figure~\ref{fig:case_study (c), it can be seen that there are more cases of people who enjoy playing chess and collecting stamps appearing in the same cluster, which is consistent with human intuition and the learned order of ``Hobby'' attribute.}
elements.sent-2411-392: This further confirms the prospects of our OCL method in discovering the implicit order information of unlabeled data with no obvious semantic order.
elements.sent-2411-393: For the ``Marital status'' attribute, the order learned by OCL is inconsistent with the original semantic order as demonstrated in Figure~\ref{fig:case_study (b).}
elements.sent-2411-394: The order learned by OCL indicates that the possible values semantically ranked first and third in ``Marital status'' should be more similar to better serve clustering.
elements.sent-2411-395: From the true clusters given by the dataset shown in Figure~\ref{fig:case_study (d), it can be found that more of the first and third values of the ``Marital status'' attribute simultaneously appear in the same clusters, which confirms the reasonableness of the learned order.}
elements.sent-2411-396: This excellent characteristic allows the users to better understand the cluster distribution of data by using the proposed OCL method.

Sub-figure (a) demonstrates the original data. Sub-figure (b) showcases the orders learned by the OCL method. Sub-figure (c) illustrates the possible values of the ``Hobby'' attribute in the clusters obtained by OCL. elements.sent-2411-399: More specifically, for $k$-prototypes (KPT), we first encode the categorical attribute values using the orders learned by the proposed OCL method and then treat the dataset as a pure numerical dataset for clustering using the $k$-means (KMS) algorithm~\cite{kms .}
elements.sent-2411-400: Actually, KPT enhanced by OCL becomes KMS with the categorical attributes encoded into numerical ones by OCL, and thus we call the enhanced version KMS+OCL.
elements.sent-2411-401: For H2H and ADC, since they are originally competent in processing ordinal attributes, we assign the orders learned by OCL to the categorical attributes, and let H2H and ADC treat all the categorical attributes as ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-402: Accordingly, their enhanced versions are named H2H+OCL and ADC+OCL, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-403: Table~\ref{table:mixed_p demonstrates the performance comparison of the above-mentioned mixed data clustering approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-404: It can be observed that OCL considerably enhances the clustering performance of the three approaches in terms of all three commonly used validity indices on AP and AC datasets.
elements.sent-2411-405: This significantly indicates that the learned orders can effectively improve the clustering performance on mixed data.
elements.sent-2411-406: It can also be observed that the improvement on the AP dataset is more obvious.
elements.sent-2411-407: The reason would be that AP contains both nominal and ordinal attributes as shown in Table~\ref{tb:detaile_infor .}
elements.sent-2411-408: This situation allows OCL to leverage its superiority in both learning orders for nominal attributes and potential corrections on the semantic orders of ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-409: In addition, a noteworthy special case can be seen on the DS dataset.
elements.sent-2411-410: Since DS has only two possible values on each of its categorical attributes, OCL cannot further optimize the orders through learning, because the order distance between the only two possible values is naturally fixed.
elements.sent-2411-411: This is why the clustering performance on the DS dataset remains unchanged after using the orders learned by OCL.
elements.sent-2411-412: This case brings us a hint that if the original semantic order of an ordinal attribute is already optimal, OCL will not have an impact on boosting the clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-413: In short, the proposed OCL is promising in being applied to more complex mixed data clustering where the attribute values originally have no order or with implicit order.

elements.sent-2411-414: To demonstrate the potential of our OCL for numerical data clustering, we compare OCL on two numerical datasets obtained from the UCI machine learning repository (\url{http://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml ).}
elements.sent-2411-415: The dataset statistics and comparative results are shown in Table~\ref{tb:additional_Numerical .}
elements.sent-2411-416: To implement OCL on these two numerical datasets, we adopt uniform discretization for the numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-417: That is, the values of each numerical attribute are divided into ten intervals of equal length, and these ten intervals are regarded as ten possible values with an order relationship.
elements.sent-2411-418: We implement OCL on the discretized numerical datasets to learn new orders for the intervals.
elements.sent-2411-419: To illustrate the effectiveness of order learning, we also preserve the original order by implementing OCL without order learning (OCL w.o.. OL).
elements.sent-2411-421: OL is a version of OCL with fixed original orders.
elements.sent-2411-422: It is equivalent to executing only the inner loop of Algorithm 1 in our paper to learn clusters.
elements.sent-2411-423: The outer loop does not make any adjustments to the order to ensure that it is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-424: The conventional K-Means clustering algorithm that utilizes the original order of numerical data by default is also compared.
elements.sent-2411-425: All the results are averaged on ten runs of the compared methods.
elements.sent-2411-426: The comparative results are shown in Table~\ref{tb:additional_Numerical .}
elements.sent-2411-427: It can be seen that the order learned by OCL can surpass the original order preserved by the K-Means and OCL w.o.. OL in general.
elements.sent-2411-428: This is because the order of a numerical attribute is not necessarily linearly related to the true clusters.
elements.sent-2411-429: For example, people with medium incomes may have happier family lives than those with very high or meager incomes.
elements.sent-2411-430: Therefore, the original order of the ``Income'' attribute may not be beneficial for forming two clusters of people with happy and unhappy family lives.
elements.sent-2411-431: In this scenario, the proper income order should be something like: medium $>$ high $>$ low, or reverse.
elements.sent-2411-432: The order learned by OCL can reflect such non-linear relationships among values, and thus yields more accurate clustering results in the corresponding situations.

elements.sent-2411-458: Due to the lack of a well-defined metric space, categorical data distributions are difficult to understand intuitively.
elements.sent-2411-459: Clustering is a popular data analysis technique suitable for understanding data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-460: However, the success of clustering often relies on reasonable distance metrics, which happen to be what categorical data naturally lack.
elements.sent-2411-461: This paper, therefore, introduces a new finding that the order relation among attribute values is the decisive factor in clustering accuracy, and is also the key to understanding categorical data clusters, because the essence of clustering is to order the clusters in terms of their admission to samples.
elements.sent-2411-462: To obtain the clustering-friendly order distance metric, we propose a new learning paradigm that allows joint learning of clusters and the orders.
elements.sent-2411-463: It alternatively partitions data into clusters based on order distance, and estimates the most likely orders according to the clusters.
elements.sent-2411-464: The algorithm achieves superior clustering accuracy with a convergence guarantee, and the learned orders facilitate the understanding of the non-intuitive categorical data from the cluster perspective.
elements.sent-2411-465: Experiments with ablation studies, significance tests, case studies, etc., have validated the efficacy of the proposed method.

elements.sent-2411-472: Clustering is a popular machine learning technique for data mining that can process and analyze datasets to automatically reveal sample distribution patterns.
elements.sent-2411-473: Since the ubiquitous categorical data naturally lack a well-defined metric space such as the Euclidean distance space of numerical data, the distribution of categorical data is usually under-represented, and thus valuable information can be easily twisted in clustering.
elements.sent-2411-474: This paper, therefore, introduces a novel order distance metric learning approach to intuitively represent categorical attribute values by learning their optimal order relationship and quantifying their distance in a line similar to that of the numerical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-475: Since subjectively created qualitative categorical values involve ambiguity and fuzziness, the order distance metric is learned in the context of clustering.
elements.sent-2411-476: Accordingly, a new joint learning paradigm is developed to alternatively perform clustering and order distance metric learning with low time complexity and a guarantee of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-477: More importantly, the learned order distance metric greatly reduces the difficulty of understanding and managing the non-intuitive categorical data.
elements.sent-2411-478: The source code is available at \href{https://github.com/DAJ0612/OCL_Source_Code {{https://github.com/DAJ0612/OCL\_Source\_Code .}}}
elements.sent-2411-479: Categorical attributes with qualitative values (e.g., $\{$lawyer, doctor, computer scientist$\}$ of an attribute ``occupation'') are common in cluster analysis of real datasets.
elements.sent-2411-480: As the categorical values are cognitive conceptual entities that cannot directly attend arithmetic operations~\textcolor{blue {\cite{intro1, CDS_VLDBJ , most existing clustering techniques proposed for numerical data are not directly applicable to the qualitative-valued categorical data~\textcolor{blue {\cite{intro4,intro3 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-481: Although some conventional encoding strategies, such as one-hot encoding, are feasible to convert categorical data into numerical data, information loss is often inevitable.
elements.sent-2411-482: Therefore, the development of categorical data clustering techniques, including clustering algorithms, distance measures, and distance metric learning approaches, continues to attract attention in data management and machine learning fields~\textcolor{blue {\cite{intro10, sccd, Geccb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-483: Categorical data clustering approaches can be roughly categorized into: 1) $k$-modes and its variants~\textcolor{blue {\cite{CDCDR,mwkm,woc that partition data samples into clusters based on certain distance measures, and 2) cluster quality assessment-based algorithms~\textcolor{blue {\cite{ICDCSCai,sbcc,ecc that adjust the sample-cluster affiliations based on certain cluster quality measures.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-484: Their performance is commonly dominated by the adopted metric space.
elements.sent-2411-485: Thus, the focus of current research has shifted from the design of clustering algorithms to proposing reasonable measures that suit categorical data clustering~\textcolor{blue {\cite{intro7 .}}}
elements.figure-2411-041: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=3.3in]{Figures/Figure1_toy_example.pdf}} \caption{\rtwo{Clustering accuracy of our method (indicated by red stars)} and the variants of $k$-modes~\textcolor{blue}{\cite{kmd}}: 1) WO: Without considering semantic Orders indicated by green bars, 2) SO: with Semantic Orders indicated by dark red bars, and 3) RO: with Random Orders generated 1000 times to characterize the clustering accuracy distribution on datasets comprising nominal attributes (blue region), ordinal attributes (purple region), and both (i.e., mixed data indicated by brown region). } \label{fig:pre_ex}
elements.sent-2411-486: The Hamming distance and one-hot encoding are common measure and encoding strategy, respectively, and they both simply represent the distance between two categorical values based on whether the values are identical or not, which is far from informative.
elements.sent-2411-487: Later, distance measures have been proposed to finely indicate the differences at the value level by considering the intra-attribute value probabilities~\textcolor{blue {\cite{emd3 , the inter-attribute dependence~\textcolor{blue {\cite{adm,cbdm_journal,abdm , or both~\textcolor{blue {\cite{jdm,udm .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-488: Advanced encoding strategies that encode distances between data samples~\textcolor{blue {\cite{sbc,GFBD , and couplings of attribute values~\textcolor{blue {\cite{cde_conf,cde,QGRL have also been proposed.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-489: Since they are clustering task independent, learning-based approaches have been further developed to optimize the representation and clustering jointly to circumvent suboptimal solutions.
elements.sent-2411-490: Typical learning-based works include the distance learning-based methods~\textcolor{blue {\cite{oc,woc,ADC that learn the dissimilarity relationship of categorical values, and representation learning-based methods~\textcolor{blue {\cite{COForest,HARR,untie that learn to project categorical attribute values into a higher-dimensional Euclidean distance space.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-491: Furthermore, when processing mixed datasets comprising both categorical and numerical attributes, these approaches inadvertently amplify the weight of categorical attributes.
elements.sent-2411-492: This introduces heterogeneity and thus compromises the reliability of cluster analysis results.
elements.sent-2411-493: To address this, some methods project categorical attribute values into one-dimensional Euclidean distance metric space based on their semantic order~\textcolor{blue {\cite{iNDIA24pr,HDC (e.g., an attribute ``decision'' with values $\{$strong\_accept, accept, \dots, strong\_reject$\}$), aiming to align them with numerical attributes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-494: However, this strategy is inapplicable to attributes lacking semantic order.
elements.sent-2411-495: Even when a semantic order exists, it is often subjectively assigned by the data collector.
elements.sent-2411-496: Consequently, these methods demonstrate significant clustering performance improvements only on datasets where the semantic order is highly relevant to the clustering task.
elements.sent-2411-497: This motivates a more general hypothesis regarding the concept of ``order'': The implicit optimal order of categorical attribute values (even in the absence of semantic order) can substantially enhance clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-498: To validate the hypothesis, a demonstration experiment (illustrated in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:pre_ex ) is conducted on twelve datasets, with statistical characteristics detailed in Table~\textcolor{blue {\ref{tb:detaile_infor within Section~\textcolor{blue {\ref{sct:exp_Set .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-499: The standard $k$-modes~\textcolor{blue {\cite{kmd clustering algorithm is employed adopting the following three distance metrics: 1) the original Hamming distance, i.e., treat categorical attributes Without Orders (WO); 2) Semantic Order (SO) distance, i.e., treat ordinal attribute values like $\{$accept, neutral, reject$\}$ with normalized order distances $dist(\text{accept ,\text{neutral )=0.5$, $dist(\text{neutral ,$ $\text{reject )=0.5$, $dist(\text{accept ,$ $\text{reject )=1$; and 3) Random Order (RO) distance, i.e., distances derived from randomly assigned orders to the values of each attribute, followed by normalization.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-500: Note that SO is inapplicable to the left four datasets, which consist solely of nominal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-501: Due to the random initialization of cluster modes, $k$-modes+WO and $k$-modes+SO are each executed 100 times, with average performance reported.
elements.sent-2411-502: Results indicate that although SO is utilized on the right 8 datasets, its performance (red bars) does not consistently surpass that of WO (green bars).
elements.sent-2411-503: This occurs because the semantic order, being subjectively defined by data collectors, may not align with the clustering task.
elements.sent-2411-504: Crucially, the 1000 trials of RO adequately traverse the state space of implicit value orders of the attributes.
elements.sent-2411-505: Thus, the hypothesis is strongly validated by the observation that the upper performance limits of RO are markedly higher than the performance of SO and WO.
elements.sent-2411-506: Therefore, the above evidence suggests that identifying the implicit optimal orders represents a critical factor for achieving accurate categorical data clustering.
elements.sent-2411-507: This paper, therefore, proposes a new order learning-based clustering paradigm that dynamically estimates orders based on the current data partition to optimize the data partition.
elements.sent-2411-508: To make full use of the learned orders, a distance learning mechanism is also introduced to dynamically update the order distance measure for the sample-cluster affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-509: Since the order learning and the clustering task are connected to form an integrated optimization problem, the sub-optimal clustering solutions can be considerably circumvented.
elements.sent-2411-510: The convergence of the proposed optimization algorithm and the metric properties of the order distance are rigorously guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-511: Extensive experiments, including comparative studies, ablation studies, significance tests, case studies, and intuitive visualization, demonstrate the superiority of the proposed paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-512: The main contributions of this work can be summarized into the following three points:.
elements.sent-2411-513: The proposed iterative learning mechanism more thoroughly optimizes the order, distance, and clusters by considering their connections.
elements.sent-2411-514: As a result, it features adaptability and is more robust to various clustering scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-515: Moreover, the algorithm is efficient, strictly convergent, and can be easily adapted for mixed data clustering.
elements.sent-2411-516: This section overviews the related works in: 1) data encoding, 2) representation learning, and 3) consideration of value order, from the perspective of categorical data clustering.
elements.sent-2411-517: The data encoding techniques convert categorical data into numerical values based on certain strategies and then cluster numerical encoding.
elements.sent-2411-518: As the conventional one-hot encoding and the Hamming distance cannot distinguish different degrees of dissimilarity~\textcolor{blue {\cite{intro6,intro10 of data samples, information loss is usually unavoidable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-519: Some metrics~\textcolor{blue {\cite{adm,cbdm_journal,abdm further define distance considering the relevance between attributes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-520: Later, the measures~\textcolor{blue {\cite{jdm,cms and encoding strategies~\textcolor{blue {\cite{cde_conf,cde,sbc that more comprehensively consider both the intra- and inter-attribute couplings reflected by the data statistics have also been presented.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-521: Recently, more advanced representation learning methods have been proposed to learn categorical data encoding, facilitating better data representation in data analysis tasks.
elements.sent-2411-522: The work proposed in~\textcolor{blue {\cite{untie projects dataset through different kernels for more informative coupling representation, and then learns the kernel parameters to obtain task-oriented representation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-523: In contrast, \textcolor{blue {\cite{ICDCSCai reveals the complex nested multi-granular cluster effect to obtain multi-granular distribution encoding representation of categorical data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-524: However, their performance is highly sensitive to hyper-parameters, making optimal selection labor-intensive across diverse real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-525: Although a parameter-free approach~\textcolor{blue {\cite{woc has been proposed to learn sample-cluster distance based on cluster statistics, it does not form a valid distance metric for categorical data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-526: Some new advances proposed with the consideration of value order further distinguish categorical attributes into nominal and ordinal attributes in clustering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-527: The distance metrics~\textcolor{blue {\cite{ ebdmjournalLLL, udm define the distance between attribute values based on the data statistical information with considering the semantic order.}}}
elements.sent-2411-528: Later, a representation learning approach was introduced by~\textcolor{blue {\cite{dlc , which treats the possible values of an ordinal attribute as linearly arranged concepts, and learns the distances between adjacent concepts.}}}
elements.sent-2411-529: Later, the works~\textcolor{blue {\cite{ADC,HDC further unify the distances of nominal and ordinal attributes, and make them learnable with clustering.}}}
elements.sent-2411-530: The most recent work~\textcolor{blue {\cite{het2hom,HARR attempts to convert the nominal attributes into ordinal ones through geometric projection, and then learn the latent distance spaces during clustering.}}}
elements.sent-2411-531: However, they all assume the availability of the semantic order of attribute values.
elements.sent-2411-532: Moreover, they directly adopt this order for clustering, and overlook that the semantic order may not be suitable under different clustering scenarios as shown in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:pre_ex .}}}
elements.sent-2411-533: This section first formulates the problem, then proposes the order distance definition, order learning mechanism, and the joint learning of the order-guided distances and clusters with analysis.
elements.sent-2411-534: Given a dataset $X=\{\mathbf{x _1,\mathbf{x _2,...,\mathbf{x _n\}$ with $n$ samples (e.g., a collection of $n$ clients of a bank).}}}
elements.sent-2411-535: Each sample $\mathbf{x _i$ can be denoted as an $s$-dimensional row vector $\mathbf{x _i=[x_{i,1 ,x_{i,2 ,..,x_{i,s ]^\top$ represented by $s$ attributes (e.g., occupation, priority, income, etc.) including $s^{\{c\}}$ categorical and $s^{\{u\}}$ numerical attributes with $s^{\{c\} +s^{\{u\} =s$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-536: For simplicity and without loss of generality, assume that all categorical and numerical attributes come from the corresponding attribute sets $A^{\{c\}}$ and $A^{\{u\}}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-537: Each attribute can be denoted as a column vector $\mathbf{a _r=[x_{1,r ,x_{2,r ,...,x_{n,r ]$ composed of the description of all $n$ samples, all taking values from a corresponding possible value set $V_r=\{v_{r,1 ,v_{r,2 ,...,v_{r,l_r}\}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-538: We use $l_r$ to indicate the total number of possible values of an attribute, and for a typical categorical attribute, $l_r$ is usually finite and satisfies $l_r<n$.
elements.sent-2411-539: Note that the subscript sequential numbers of possible values only distinguish them, and do not indicate their order.
elements.sent-2411-540: We do not distinguish between nominal and ordinal attributes under categorical attributes in the whole Section~\textcolor{blue {\ref{sct:PM , because their order will be learned uniformly by the proposed method.}}}
elements.sent-2411-541: The difference is that there exists ambiguity in the relationship among categorical possible values, so their potential optimal order should be learned in a specific context, i.e., clustering here.
elements.sent-2411-542: We denote the optimal order of $\mathbf{a _r$ as $O^*_r=\{o^*(v_{r,1 ),o^*(v_{r,2 ),...,o^*(v_{r,l_r )\}$ where the superscript ``$*$'' indicates the optimum and the value of $o^*(v_{r,g )$ is an integer reflecting the unique ranking of $v_{r,g}$ among all its $l_r$ possible values.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-543: For a categorical $\mathbf{a _r\in A^{\{c\}}$, by still taking the ``occupation'' attribute as an example, if its three possible values $v_{r,1 =\text{lawyer}$, $v_{r,2 =\text{doctor}$, and $v_{r,3 =\text{scientist}$, ranking second, third, and first, respectively, then the corresponding order is $O_r=\{o(\text{lawyer ),o(\text{doctor ),o(\text{scientist )\}=\{2,3,1\}$, which reflects value relationship satisfying: $\text{dist (v_{r,g ,v_{r,h )\propto |o(v_{r,g )-o(v_{r,h )|$.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-557: OLO solves a circuit routing cost optimization.
elements.sent-2411-558: It assumes that there are a set of $\varsigma$ pins with $\varsigma(\varsigma-1)/2$ wire connections, and there are $\varsigma$ holes in a line with adjacent holes at unit distances apart to put the $\varsigma$ pins.
elements.sent-2411-559: If the number of wires connecting the pins is given, OLO can arrange the pins into holes to minimize the overall wire length to save cost.
elements.sent-2411-560: Intuitively, the solution follows a unimodal arrangement, where the pins that are more densely connected with the other pins (i.e., with more wire linkages) are placed in the middle holes.
elements.sent-2411-561: Corresponding to our problem, how to define the number of wires between the pins, i.e., the link density between possible values within the same cluster, is the prerequisite to implement OLO.

elements.sent-2411-584: The whole algorithm named Order and Cluster Learning (OCL) is summarized as Algorithm~\textcolor{blue {\ref{alg:OCL .}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{OCL: Order and Cluster Learning Algorithm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \label{alg:OCL} \REQUIRE Dataset $X$, number of \rtwo{sought} clusters $k$ \ENSURE Partition $\mathbf{Q}$, order $O$ \STATE Initialization: Set outer loop counter and convergence mark by $E\leftarrow 0$ and $Conv\_O\leftarrow False$, respectively; Randomly partition $X$ into $k$ clusters $C$, obtain $\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$ and $\mathbf{P}^{\{I\}}$ accordingly; Randomly initialize $O^{\{E\}}$, update $\mathbf{D}^{\{E\}}$ accordingly; \WHILE{$Conv\_O=False$} \STATE $E\leftarrow E+1$; Compute $O^{\{E\}}$ by Eqs.~(\textcolor{blue}{\ref{eq:relax_order}}) - (\textcolor{blue}{\ref{eq:weight_mean}}); Update $\mathbf{D}^{\{E\}}$ accordingly; Set inner loop counter and convergence mark by $I\leftarrow 0$ and $Conv\_I\leftarrow False$, respectively; \WHILE{$Conv\_I=False$} \STATE $I\leftarrow I+1$; Compute $\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$ by Eq.~(\textcolor{blue}{\ref{eq:qim}}); Update $\mathbf{P}^{\{I\}}$ accordingly; Obtain $L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}},O^{\{E\}})$; \IF {$L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}},O^{\{E\}})\geq L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{I-1\}},O^{\{E\}})$} \STATE $Conv\_I\leftarrow True$; $\mathbf{Q}^{\{E\}}=\mathbf{Q}^{\{I\}}$; \ENDIF \ENDWHILE \IF {$L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{E\}},O^{\{E\}})\geq L(\mathbf{Q}^{\{E-1\}},O^{\{E-1\}})$} \STATE $Conv\_O\leftarrow True$; \ENDIF \ENDWHILE \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-623: Table \textcolor{blue {\ref{tb:ab_ca compares the three OCL variants.}}}
elements.sent-2411-624: More specifically, OCL outperforms OCL-I on 17, 14, 17, and 17 out of 20 datasets in terms of CA, ARI, NMI, and CMP, respectively, indicating that OD effectively leverages the distribution of attribute values across clusters for more reasonable order distance measurement.
elements.sent-2411-625: Furthermore, OCL-I outperforms OCL-II on 17, 16, 15, and 13 out of 20 datasets in terms of CA, ARI, NMI, and CMP, respectively, verifying the effectiveness of the OL mechanism in searching for optimal orders during clustering.
elements.sent-2411-626: In addition, compared to OCL-III, which completely disregards OI, OCL-II achieves better performance on 16, 18, 17, and 19 out of 20 datasets in terms of CA, ARI, NMI, and CMP, respectively, demonstrating that even a roughly estimated initial order can effectively improve clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-627: From the data aspect, to validate our hypothesis that correct order information is crucial to categorical data clustering, ablation experiments are conducted on datasets containing both nominal and ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-628: The OCL that learns order for both nominal and ordinal attributes is compared with its two variants: (1) LNRO (Learn Nominal Reserve Ordinal), which retains the semantic order of the original ordinal attribute and only learns orders for the nominal attributes, and (2) RNRO (Reserve Nominal Reserve Ordinal), which preserves the original semantic order for ordinal attributes without learning, and similarly imposes no order learning for nominal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-629: Table~\textcolor{blue {\ref{tb:ab_ca_d compares the clustering performance of the three variants in CA, ARI, and NMI indices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-630: It can be observed that, in terms of CA and ARI, on seven out of the nine datasets, LNRO outperforms RNRO.
elements.sent-2411-631: AS for the NMI and CMP indices, LNRO outperforms RNRO on six out of nine datasets, the performance gap between LNRO and RNRO is tiny, which can still verify the effectiveness of learning orders for nominal attributes in general.
elements.sent-2411-632: Moreover, we can also find that the OCL outperforms LNRO on eight out of the nine datasets in CA and ARi indices, and seven out of the nine datasets in NMI and CMP indices.
elements.sent-2411-633: The convergence of OCL is evaluated by executing it on the SB, NS, and AP datasets, and present the convergence curves in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:conv_curve_all .}}}
elements.sent-2411-634: Moreover, for all the tested datasets, OCL completes the learning process within 40 iterations with at most three updates of the orders.
elements.sent-2411-635: This reflects that OCL converges efficiently with guarantee, which conforms to the analysis in Section~\textcolor{blue {\ref{sct:theoretical_analysis .}}}

elements.sent-2411-636: All sample values are randomly generated from five possible values per attribute.
elements.sent-2411-637: Overall, Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:time_complex shows that OCL achieves higher efficiency than state-of-the-art methods while maintaining comparable scalability to traditional baseline methods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-638: More specifically, on the synthetic datasets, OCL maintains a nearly linear growth rate in execution time, which is consistent with the time complexity analysis in Theorem~\textcolor{blue {\ref{the:time_analysis , and its efficiency outperforms: (1) UDMC, AMPHM, and DLC on large-scale datasets; (2) HARR, UDMC, AMPHM, and CBDM on high-dimensional datasets; \mrone{(3) the same competitors on datasets with numerous clusters.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-639: In addition, the evaluation is also conducted on the real benchmark NS dataset with varying sampling rates (from 50\% to 500\% in steps of 50\%) to show that OCL achieves scalability without compromising clustering accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-640: In summary, OCL is effective and efficient compared to state-of-the-art methods, without incurring much additional computational cost in comparison with the traditional simple approaches.

elements.sent-2411-641: To demonstrate the cluster discrimination ability of the OCL method, We use OCL, CBDM, and ADC to learn a distance metric for the VT dataset, then compute a new distance matrix for the data samples, which are projected into 2-D space for visualization via t-SNE~\textcolor{blue {\cite{r1visual , as shown in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:Visualization_tsne_Vt .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-642: We use different markers (distinguished in colors and shapes) to indicate ground-truth clusters, with point positions representing the samples projected into the 2-D space by t-SNE.
elements.sent-2411-643: This effectively visualizes how well each method captures the distinguishable cluster distributions for intuitive comparison.
elements.sent-2411-644: It can be seen from Figure \textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:Visualization_tsne_Vt that OCL has significantly better cluster discrimination ability, as it performs clustering task-oriented distance learning to better suit the exploration of the $k^*$ clusters.}}}
elements.sent-2411-645: More specifically, for KPT, we first encode the categorical attribute values using the orders learned by the proposed OCL method and then treat the dataset as a pure numerical dataset for clustering using the $k$-means (KMS) algorithm~\textcolor{blue {\cite{kms .}}}
elements.sent-2411-646: Actually, KPT enhanced by OCL is equivalent to KMS with the categorical attributes encoded into numerical ones by OCL, and thus we call the enhanced version KMS+OCL.
elements.sent-2411-647: For HARR and ADC, since they are originally competent in processing ordinal attributes, the orders learned by OCL are assigned to the categorical attributes, and we let HARR and ADC treat all the categorical attributes as ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-648: Table~\textcolor{blue {\ref{table:mixed_p demonstrates the performance of the above-mentioned approaches and their OCL-enhanced versions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-649: It can be observed that OCL considerably enhances the clustering performance of the three approaches in terms of AP and AC datasets.
elements.sent-2411-650: The improvement on the AP dataset is more obvious because the AP contains both nominal and ordinal attributes as shown in Table~\textcolor{blue {\ref{tb:detaile_infor .}}}
elements.sent-2411-651: This situation allows OCL to leverage its superiority in learning orders for nominal attributes and potential corrections on the semantic orders of ordinal attributes.
elements.sent-2411-652: A noteworthy special case can be seen on the DS dataset.
elements.sent-2411-653: Since DS has only two possible values on each of its attributes, OCL cannot further optimize the orders.
elements.sent-2411-654: This observation suggests that when the original semantic order of an ordinal attribute is already optimal, OCL exerts no measurable effect on clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-655: In short, the proposed OCL is promising for more complex mixed-data clustering scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-056: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=3.2in]{Figures/case_study.pdf}} \caption{A case study on HR dataset. ``Hobby'' is a nominal attribute with possible values $\{$chess, sport, stamp$\}$, and the remaining attributes are ordinal attributes with the semantic order of their possible values indicated by $\{$1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th$\}$. Sub-figure (a) demonstrates the original data. Sub-figure (b) showcases the orders learned by the OCL method. Sub-figure (c) illustrates the possible values of the ``Hobby'' attribute in the clusters obtained by OCL. Sub-figure (d) presents the possible values of the ``Marital status'' attribute in the true clusters indicated by the data labels.} \label{fig:case_study}
elements.sent-2411-656: The HR dataset with one nominal and three ordinal attributes records personal information, such as the nominal ``Hobby'' and the ordinal ``Education level'', ``Age'', and ``Marital status''.
elements.sent-2411-657: The true labels divide the samples into three groups of club members for psychological evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-658: The original dataset information is visualized in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:case_study (a).}}}
elements.sent-2411-659: It can be seen from Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:case_study (b) that OCL can correctly learn the semantic order of the values from attributes ``Education level'' and ``Age'', as the learned orders are consistent with the original semantic orders.}}}
elements.sent-2411-660: Moreover, for the nominal attribute ``Hobby'', although there is no original obvious semantic order, the learned order is very meaningful in data analysis and knowledge acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-661: First, the learned order distance between ``chess'' and ``stamp collection'' hobbies is closer, while the distance between ``stamp collection'' and ``sport'' is larger.
elements.sent-2411-662: This is consistent with cognitive intuition that ``chess'' is a quieter sport, so it ranks between the quieter hobby ``stamp collection'' and its sibling ``sport''.
elements.sent-2411-663: As shown in the clustering results obtained by OCL in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:case_study (c), it can be seen that there are more cases of people who enjoy playing chess and collecting stamps appearing in the same cluster, which is consistent with human intuition and the learned order of ``Hobby'' attribute.}}}
elements.sent-2411-664: For the ``Marital status'' attribute, the order learned by OCL is inconsistent with the original semantic order as demonstrated in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:case_study (b).}}}
elements.sent-2411-665: From the true clusters given by the dataset shown in Figure~\textcolor{blue {\ref{fig:case_study (d), it can be found that more of the first and third values of the ``Marital status'' attribute simultaneously appear in the same clusters, which confirms the reasonableness of the learned order.}}}
elements.sent-2411-666: This characteristic allows the users to better understand the cluster distribution of data, or even explore deeper meanings and relationships of values.
elements.sent-2411-667: Accordingly, the OCL paradigm is proposed, allowing joint learning of data partitions and order relation-guided distances.
elements.sent-2411-668: OCL converges quickly with a guarantee, and extensive experiments on 20 real benchmark datasets fully demonstrate its superiority in comparison with the state-of-the-art clustering methods.
elements.sent-2411-669: Moreover, it would be promising to extend OCL into more complex scenarios, e.g., processing non-stationary mixed dataset with unknown number of imbalanced clusters.
